sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 15, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: After SPACE, the left target frame at rows 28-31 is still present (unchanged color 'c'). The white block is at row 36 only. The player is at green rows 37-39, cols 16-19. The target frame didn't activate (didn't change to 'G') because the player wasn't close enough. The first collection worked because the white block overlapped with the target frame.
- Goal model: Collect all three blue-square targets by moving adjacent and pressing SPACE.
- Action model: Movement moves player 4 cells. SPACE collects adjacent targets.
- Plan: Try moving UP to get the white block overlapping with the left target frame at rows 28-31.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 14 of about 71 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
UP didn't change anything (board_changed: false). The player is blocked from moving UP. Let me reconsider the mechanic.
Looking at the current state more carefully:
- Green at rows 37-39, cols 16-19
- White at row 36, cols 16-19
- G frame at rows 32-35, cols 16-19 (this is the "activated" frame from the second SPACE)
- Blue center at rows 33-34, cols 17-18 (inside the G frame)
- Left target frame still at rows 28-31, cols 16-19
The player can't move UP because the G frame (rows 32-35) is blocking the way. The white block is at row 36, and above it is the G frame at rows 32-35, and above that is the left target at rows 28-31.
So the issue is that the second SPACE created a G frame that's now blocking the player from reaching the left target. This is a problem.
Wait, let me reconsider what happened. Maybe the second SPACE DID collect something, but it collected the carried blue item instead of the target. The G frame at rows 32-35 might be the "collected" version of the carried item.
Actually, I think I need to reconsider the whole mechanic. Let me think about what the first SPACE did:
1. Player was at green rows 41-43, white rows 36-40
2. Target frame was at rows 36-39, which had changed to 'G' (activated)
3. After SPACE: white block expanded to include the frame area, blue center was inside
And the second SPACE:
1. Player was at green rows 37-39, white rows 32-36
2. Left target frame at rows 28-31 was still 'c' (not activated)
3. After SPACE: white block shrank to row 36, G frame appeared at rows 32-35
Hmm, so the second SPACE didn't collect the left target because it wasn't activated. Instead, it seems like the SPACE just changed the carried item's appearance.
The problem is that now there's a G frame blocking the player from reaching the left target. I need to find a way to get past it.
Options:
1. Move DOWN to get away from the G frame, then approach from a different direction
2. Move RIGHT or LEFT to go around the G frame
3. Try SPACE again to see if it does something different
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider. The G frame is at rows 32-35, cols 16-19. The left target is at rows 28-31, cols 16-19. They're in the same column range. The player can't move UP through the G frame.
But maybe I can move RIGHT to go around the G frame, then move UP to the left target's row, then move LEFT to approach the target from the right side.
Let me try: RIGHT to move away from the G frame, then UP to get to the target's row level, then LEFT to approach the target.
Actually, let me first check if the player can move RIGHT. The player is at cols 16-19. Moving RIGHT would put the player at cols 20-23.
Let me try moving RIGHT, then UP, then LEFT to approach the left target from a different angle.
Wait, actually, let me reconsider the whole approach. Maybe the mechanic isn't about collecting targets by moving to them. Let me look at the big picture:
- There are 3 target frames with blue centers
- The player has a green block and a white block
- SPACE seems to do something with the white block and nearby objects
- The first SPACE "collected" the bottom-center target
- The second SPACE didn't collect the left target
Maybe the issue is that I need to approach the target from a specific direction. In the first case, the player approached from below (moved UP to reach the target). For the left target, the player also approached from below but couldn't get close enough because the G frame is blocking.
Let me try a different approach: move DOWN to clear the G frame, then move RIGHT to go around, then UP and LEFT to approach the target from the right side.
Actually, I realize I might be overcomplicating this. Let me just try moving RIGHT to get past the G frame, then UP to the target's level, then LEFT to approach it.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: UP is blocked by the G frame at rows 32-35. The left target at rows 28-31 is still uncollected. The G frame is blocking upward movement. Need to approach from a different direction.
Plan: Move RIGHT to go around the G frame, then UP to the target's row, then LEFT to approach the left target from the side.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Move RIGHT to get past the blocking G frame, then UP, then LEFT to approach target from side
# Current: green rows 37-39, cols 16-19
# Target: rows 28-31, cols 16-19
# G frame blocking: rows 32-35, cols 16-19
# Strategy: RIGHT to cols 20-23, UP to rows ~28-31, LEFT back to cols 16-19
action(['RIGHT', 'UP', 'UP', 'LEFT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
